posts.name: Matthew Hemming
posts.cooked:
I was thrilled to join this exceptional dinner last week. The germ of this idea was planted when I served the organiser a bottle of 1905 Figeac a couple of years ago. I also helped him find the 1961 but he did all the rest, with his customary aplomb.

After a magnum of very fresh, late disgorged 1982 Moet we got started on a marathon line up of Figeac…

The first 3 were blind.

2004
Touch fudgey, broad and forward. Fairly low acid and, despite nice mid-palate spice, this felt marked by a warm vintage. I suspected 2005. 16/20

2005
This had huge energy and drive. Fantastic mid-palate volume and literally hums with energy. Fruit is taut, crisp and well defined. Classy and cool on the finish. 18/20

2006
Still oaky and primary. Very youthful but this is trying too hard. A bit too worked and extracted. Almost Super Tuscan in profile. 16.5/20

1990
Rich, generous and spicy. The palate shows great lift and cut. Shows the Figeac earthiness. Strong mineral core. 18.5/20 - a very smart 1990.

1989
More immediately fruity than the 90 but less spine and structure. Actually lacks some intensity. Loose on the finish. 16.5/20

1985
Just slightly corky and also showing some oxidative notes. Weak. -

1983
Secondary aromas with notes of cinnamon and leather. Just a touch attenuated. Very pretty but slight. 16.5/20

1981
Mature and slightly mushroomy. Bit simple and soft. On the downslope. 14.5/20

1979 - magnum
Crisp red berry fruit with a hint of Figeac leafiness. Smart, with vegetal edge lending complexity. Mid-palate a touch resinous, detracting from whole. 16/20

1978
Scalped by TCA. -

1975
Simply outstanding. Masses of dark fruit and then a wine of energy, spice and precision. Shows the Pomerol character of Figeac. 19/20

1974
Faint, delicate nose. Light and a bit dilute. Just hanging in there but definitely a weaker rendition. 14.5/20

1971
Flamboyant, rich and explosive. Great colour and perfume. Lacks a bit of magic on finish which has a ferrous note. A bit coarse. 17.5/20

1970
Relaxed, composed and assured. A wine of complexity and confidence. Savoury, compact and classy. 17/20

1966
Surprisingly youthful. Vital, spicy and cool in profile. Lots on the attack but them falls away just a touch. Lovely spice on the very back end. 17.5/20

1964
Rich and broad. Large scaled. Lovely spice on the mid-palate but a touch over generous, spilling into looseness. 16.5/20

1962
Slightly oxidative and tiring with a hint of cork. Elegant and sweet but the VA is on the edge. Not really right. 16/20??

1961
Massive colour. Has depth and density of dark-toned fruit. Pristine and glistening. Truly superb, with fabulous structure. 19/20

1959
A big wine on the borderline of being fudgey. Has breadth, density and spice. A vinous finish injects vitality and rescues it. 17/20

1953
Gorgeous scent of ginger and spice, with a briary/woodsy note of tobacco. Fragrant at first but then becomes more taut and mineral. Coalesces in the glass. Brilliant. 17.5/20

1949
Stunning colour. Taut, crisp and fresh with bright fruit but lacks some complexity and volume. 16.5/20

1945 - negociant bottling
Broad, rich and dense. Shows power, weight and richness of vintage. Just starting to fray at edges but very impressive still. 17/20

1911
At first I really liked the high-toned, sweet perfume. Palate was shot, acidic and dried out though. 13.5/20

1905
Red fruit perfume. Silky, delicate and fully resolved. Very pretty but absolutely not powerful.17/20

posts.name: Faryan Amir-Ghassemi
posts.cooked:
This is fantastic. 75 Figeac was a revelation to me several years ago but has shown inconsistent. Good to hear the 05 is progressing well. You missed the 98!

Cheers

posts.name: Matthew Hemming
posts.cooked: We also missed the 1982 because of a failed delivery!

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
What a fantastic tasting, and thank you for the notes.

Agree on the 1975, that it is a great wine, but side by side against a magnum of 1970, preferred the 1970. But it’s close.

The good news is that you have plenty of wine for another tasting, 2000, 1998, 1995, 1982 etc.

posts.cooked: That’s nice to read. I just bought a magnum of the '75 but haven’t opened it yet.

posts.cooked: My lordy, that’s just awesome. Thanks for sharing. Any pics?

posts.name: Craig Gleason
posts.cooked: Robert and I did two vintages last year (78 and 90). One of them was corked

posts.name: Matthew King
posts.cooked:
Matthew:
Great read.

I had to chuckle when you wrote the 1905 was fully resolved. I should hope so!

What hallmarks define a Figeac wine? I don’t have much experience with them.

I will say they have a beautiful label …

posts.name: Blake Brown
posts.cooked: Great tasting and a once in a lifetime event as it would be for any chateau. Thanks for the nice, succinct notes as well.
What fun that must have been! Thanks for posting

posts.name: Matthew Hemming
posts.cooked:
What hallmarks define a Figeac wine? I don’t have much experience with them.

I actually find this quite a tricky question. Figeac is often said to be a paradigm of traditional St Emilion but I just don’t think that’s correct. They may not be a modernist St Emilion but they’re not exactly classic. The planting of the vineyards to some 30% Merlot then equal parts of the two Cabernets can give a Medocain character. The wines are sometimes earthy, dusty or even leafy. They’re not particularly expressive when young and the pronounced minerality can seem stern and almost hard. At the same time, the vineyardsits atop an extension of Pomerol’s gravel plateau, along with Cheval Blanc, that can give Figeac a distinctly Pomerol-like edge. So, there are two opposing influences pulling the wine towards both Pomerol and the Medoc…I find Figeac very distinctive, but traditional St Emilion? Not really.

posts.name: Jayson Cohen
posts.cooked:
As suggested, Figeac until very recently was a unique but traditionally made wine. It typically needed a lot of time but aged into a very complex mineral wine with no shortage to my palate of mulberry-cranberry fruit. Very dynamic mouthfeel. Crunchy for Bordeaux.

The recent turn toward the dark side is disheartening.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
I’ve read this several times, just a killer tasting and very informative.

I’m certainly no expert here, but I’ve never really associated this wine with a Pomerol edge, but it is (or was, sadly), quite distinct for St Em. Figeac’s higher concentration of Cab Franc shines through to me, with more of a red fruit, higher acid, dry earth profile. Some ripe vintages like 1990 are excellent because of the higher acid, but that vintage to me is not the norm.

posts.name: Matthew King
posts.cooked:
Matthew Hemming:

What hallmarks define a Figeac wine? I don’t have much experience with them.

I actually find this quite a tricky question. Figeac is often said to be a paradigm of traditional St Emilion but I just don’t think that’s correct. They may not be a modernist St Emilion but they’re not exactly classic. The planting of the vineyards to some 30% Merlot then equal parts of the two Cabernets can give a Medocain character. The wines are sometimes earthy, dusty or even leafy. They’re not particularly expressive when young and the pronounced minerality can seem stern and almost hard. At the same time, the vineyardsits atop an extension of Pomerol’s gravel plateau, along with Cheval Blanc, that can give Figeac a distinctly Pomerol-like edge. So, there are two opposing influences pulling the wine towards both Pomerol and the Medoc…I find Figeac very distinctive, but traditional St Emilion? Not really.

Thanks for thoughtful response. I like these types of yin yang wines …

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
I have been a Figeac lover for many years, and lucky enough to have gone through a few verticals, though nothing pre-war actually pre-wars like this.


For me, Figeac because of the Cabernet Sauvignon, is probably the last Right Bank wine to ripen completely, and that means that it often comes with a green edges as some part of the cepage did not manage to get fully ripe. Sometimes, it comes across as asparagus (1979) sometimes a little bell pepper (2000). Most of the time, there is a faint green edge to the wine, great acidity, and the fruit has a floral element, an essence of roses, and the minerality Matthew mentioned.

It is a wine that really needs to be cellared for a long term. Like Cheval Blanc, it is also very hard to really gauge when it’s young.The question as to whether it is a typical Saint Emilion is made more difficult, when one realizes how different the wines around Pavie are to the wines neighboring Pomerol. Even if you confine the comparison to those wines, and those years where the Cab Sauvignon got fully ripe, 1998, 2009 and 2015, they were still very different from its neighbors.

posts.name: Graeme Gee
posts.cooked:
24 bottles, three compromised by cork. Sounds about right!
Graeme

posts.name: Rhodes Kriete
posts.cooked:
